Week 2: Data Modeling and Database Normalization

Agenda:

  • Data Modeling: The Conceptual Stage and Entity Relationship Diagrams
  • Data Modeling: The logical stage and database normalization: 1st, 2nd, and 3rd normal forms
  • Data Modeling: the physical stage
  • Pipeline Project: next steps
  • Week 2 assignment

What is data modeling?

Data modeling can be defined as the process of creating a structured blueprint that defines how data will be organized and how it is stored. It translates business requirements into a defined specification, ensuring data is efficiently structured to support an organization's operational and analytical needs. goals and purposes:
  1. business goals drive the process
  2. efficiency and data integrity
  3. common understanding of the data
So, the modeling begins and ends with the business in mind.

Data modeling stage: overview

  • Conceptual Stage: The what - "what information are we tracking?"
  • Logical Stage: The how - "how is the data defined?"
  • Physical Stage: The where - "how is the data physically implemented?"
Data modeling: a walkthrough

The conceptual stage and the pipeline scenario

The conceptual stage starts with understanding entities. An entity is a thing, either real or conceptual, that has 3 fundamental properties: 1) it has independent existence, 2) each occurrence is unique, and 3) it has properties or attributes. The goal of the conceptual stage is to identify the entities and their relationships. After they have been identified, their important attributes are identified.

The logical stage and database normalization

The goal of the logical stage is to organize the entities into a formal structure using the rules of normalization Database normalization is a systematic process of organizing data into tables and columns to eliminate redundancy and prevent data anomalies. It follows a series of rules (normal forms) that progressively refine the database structure to ensure data integrity, reduce storage requirements, and create maintainable systems that can adapt to changing business needs. refer to the Normalization Summary pdf document in the week 2 resources.

normalization walkthrough

refer to the normalization summary pdf document in the week 2 resource

Pipeline Project: Next steps

  1. examining the API: the relationship between the /poll, /fetch, and /submit endpoints.
  2. required data layout.
  3. separate incomplete records from complete records.

Getting data from the api

getting data from the api is done using 4 endpoints:
  1. /static-data
  2. /poll
  3. /fetch
  4. /commit
/static-data is just what it says. It retrieves a set of data that doesn't change. You can hit it multiple times, but you will always get the data. This should lead you to consider how you deal with the data from that endpoint. /poll, /fetch, and /commmit all work in conjuntion with each other to get the visit data. Visits are continually added to the backend database (not yet, but that will start soon). This mean you have to fetch data more than once. The question then becomes, after each fetch, how do you know where you left off and where you need to begin your next fetch? You could just start over from the beginning every single time, but you would continually be getting data you already have. Then you would need a process in your code to filter the repeated data. Also, each time you fetch, it will take longer and longer. There are two reasons for this: First, there is more data to get. Second, and more importantly, you can get only 10 visits at a time. So, you need a way to mark your location so that when you fetch, you start where you left off. For example, if the hightest visit_id of your previous fetch was 1439, your next fetch should start with visit_id 1440. How do you do this? There are a couple of ways.
  1. First, you could track that number somehow. You could write it to a file and then read the file every time you do a fetch. But, what happens if you lose the file?
  2. Second, you can let the api do it.
The api is designed to track your progress in fetching data. It does this by using two endpoints: /poll and /commit

the /poll endpoint

1. What you need to use it: The /poll endpoint requires your api key and your consumer key. Your api key is required because the endpoint is protected. Your consumer key is required because the database uses it to track your progress. 2. How it works: When you access the /poll endpoint you will get data that looks likes this: 
{
  "consumer_key": "cXqFzZGdkd1dCiKs7DayE95Ybe1tsjIb",
  "committed_event_id": 0,
  "latest_event_id": 30,
  "has_new_events": true,
  "start_event_id": 1,
  "end_event_id": 30
}
Your progress is tracked, and you fetch data, by events, not by visits. Each time a visit is added, an event is generated and writes to the database. The above information is fairly straightforward to interpret: 
committed_event_id is the last event you processed
latest_event_id is the most recent event written to the database
has_new_events tells you if there is new data since your last fetch
start_event_id is the event you need to start with in your next fetch
end_event_id is the last event you need to process to be current.
3. How to use it: This tells you every single thing you need to know to continually get new data as it is added to the backend without having to track it yourself. NOTE: be attentive to the return data from the /poll endpoint. It is different when there are no new visits since your last fetch.

the /commit endoint.

1. what you need to use it: The /commit endpoint requires your api key, your consumer key, and the event_id of the last event you processed. Your api key is required because the endpoint is protected. Your consumer key is required because the database uses it to update your progress. 2. how it works: the api sends your consumer key and event_id to the database and the database updates the appropriate tables.

the /fetch endpoint

The /fetch endpoint is used to get visit data from the api. As stated above when discussing the /poll endpoint, fetching is done by event_ids, not visit ids. The swagger docs tell you what information you need to provide
The start_event_id and end_event_id values are provided in the data returned from the /poll api.

the fetch cycle

Putting this altogether, the fetch cycle is a three step process:
  1. use the /poll endpoint to check if new visits have been added
  2. fetch data from the /fetch endpoint using the data from the /poll endpoint (start_event_id and end_event_id)
  3. commit the latest event_id you have processed using /commit.

NOTES

  1. as mentioned above, you can fetch only 10 visits at a time. If you have more than 10 visits to fetch, you will need to figure out how to do that in your code.
  2. when sending data to the api, your api key is part of the headers
  3. when sending data to the api, your consumer_key and whatever event_ids are required are sent as part of the url.
  4. you can use the swagger docs to determine how the url is formed. After you fill out the data and select execute, the response data displays the url that was generated. You can use this to determine how to format it in your code.

updated requirement

The stakeholder tells you that the only data they want is clean data. This means data with no missing values. As part of your code you will need to determine how to do the following:
  1. identify unclean code. For this project, unclean code is defined as any data associated with a visit that has a null value.
  2. filter out the unclean data for when further processing is done.
  3. what to do with the unclean data. Question: should it simply be discarded?
